metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether NUL-byte detection is in scope and, if so, verify the chosen backend can actually match binary-file content (opengrep silently skips binary files; `generic` is not in `_LANG_EXTENSIONS`); if no working backend exists, drop NUL bytes from this plan per the same YAGNI reasoning applied to the truncation/pasted-text detectors.
findings.finding: NUL-byte detection is bundled into the plan as an opengrep/engine_b detector, but opengrep does not process binary files natively, 'generic' is absent from _LANG_EXTENSIONS (so a no-language rule would be skipped as unsupported_lang or silently produce no matches on binary content), and no existing codebase path handles binary-file scanning via this pipeline. Building NUL-byte detection here is both YAGNI (no grounded use-case beyond the plan assertion) and a golden-hammer application of the opengrep backend to a binary-file problem it is not designed for.
findings.location: ## What — 'flags VCS conflict markers (<<<<<<<, =======, >>>>>>>) and obvious encoding corruption (NUL bytes)'
findings.suggested_fix: Scope the DET detector to conflict markers only (opengrep `pattern-regex` on a no-language rule, applied to text files, already works via the existing pipeline). Drop the NUL-byte clause, or, if NUL-byte detection is a genuine requirement, open a separate plan that evaluates the right detection mechanism (e.g., a lightweight Python file-read pass as a new backend, not opengrep).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether opengrep/semgrep scan files containing NUL bytes or skip them as binary; if they skip, either drop the NUL-byte target from scope or specify an alternative detection mechanism (e.g. a separate Python-based detector, or a `file_globs`-scoped grep-style check).
findings.finding: The plan includes 'NUL bytes' as a detection target but does not verify that opengrep/semgrep can match NUL bytes inside files. opengrep and semgrep pre-screen for binary files (files containing NUL bytes) and typically SKIP them rather than scanning them — a regex pattern matching `\x00` will likely never fire because the file is excluded before the rule runs.
findings.location: ## What — 'obvious encoding corruption (NUL bytes)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the NUL-byte detection feasibility gap: either drop it from scope (the plan already notes 'fuzzy DSO parts' are dropped as FP-prone; NUL bytes are also a binary-file problem incompatible with the opengrep backend) or route it to a separate Python raw-bytes scan step and document that as a distinct integration path requiring a SPIKE.
findings.finding: NUL-byte detection is bundled with conflict markers in the same opengrep/engine_b detector, but opengrep (a source-code AST/regex scanner) does not process binary files: it skips them silently. A `pattern-regex` for `\x00` or `\u0000` in a detector YAML will never fire on a binary file via the opengrep backend, making the NUL-byte capability asserted by the plan infeasible through this integration path without a new backend or a separate Python scan step.
findings.location: ## What — 'obvious encoding corruption (NUL bytes)'
findings.suggested_fix: Drop NUL-byte detection from the DET criterion scope entirely (it is already weakly motivated — 'obvious encoding corruption' with no grounded use-case) and confine the detector to conflict markers only, which ARE text patterns in source files and fit the opengrep backend naturally.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the conflict-marker detector's `languages` declaration and verify it passes engine_b's `_is_applicable` gate: if using `languages: [generic]`, add `'generic'` to `_LANG_EXTENSIONS` (or use a glob-based `file_globs` with no `languages` key) so the rule is not silently skipped as `unsupported_lang`.
findings.finding: The plan declares the detector will be 'polyglot' (all file types) via the Engine B / opengrep backend, but engine_b._is_applicable skips any detector whose declared language maps to an empty extension set — and 'generic' is absent from _LANG_EXTENSIONS. A rule that declares `languages: [generic]` is silently skipped as `unsupported_lang`; a rule that omits languages entirely avoids the skip but opengrep's text-regex match behavior without a language hint across heterogeneous file types (compiled binaries, images, etc.) is unverified in the codebase. The plan must confirm which language declaration is used and that the routing gate passes.
findings.location: ## What — 'polyglot' + ## Scope — 'A builtin detector rule'
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) declare no `languages` key (routes as always-applicable; opengrep handles generic text matching) and add an integration test asserting the detector matches a .py, .go, and .txt file containing `<<<<<<<`; or (b) add `"generic": ()` to `_LANG_EXTENSIONS` with a special-case in `_is_applicable` for the empty-extension polyglot case. Option (a) is the lightest path, matching how the metric backend's detectors work.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the language-declaration strategy for the polyglot conflict-marker detector: either (a) omit `languages:` entirely (engine_b always-applicable path, per `_is_applicable` line 210-211) or (b) add `generic` to `_LANG_EXTENSIONS` — document the choice explicitly so the implementer does not default to `languages: [generic]` which currently routes to `unsupported_lang`.
findings.finding: The plan declares the conflict-marker detector 'polyglot' but does not specify how the opengrep/engine_b language-routing will achieve that. `engine_b._is_applicable` skips any detector whose declared `languages:` list has no entry in `_LANG_EXTENSIONS` (returns `unsupported_lang`). The semgrep/opengrep `generic` language — the standard polyglot mode — is NOT in `_LANG_EXTENSIONS`, so `languages: [generic]` would be silently skipped on every repo. The only correct mechanisms are: (a) declare no `languages:` key at all (engine_b line 210-211: 'A detector that declares neither always applies'), or (b) add `generic` to `_LANG_EXTENSIONS`. The plan names neither.
findings.location: What section — 'a builtin detector rule'; AC line 3 — 'Detector is polyglot'
findings.suggested_fix: In the detector YAML, omit the `languages:` key entirely. Engine B's `_is_applicable` treats a detector with no declared languages and no declared paths as always-applicable (line 210-211: 'A detector that declares neither always applies, e.g. metric detectors'). This is the correct polyglot strategy for a text-pattern detector and avoids any `_LANG_EXTENSIONS` dependency.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the polyglot routing mechanism against `engine_b._is_applicable()`: either add `'generic'` to `_LANG_EXTENSIONS` with a broad extension set, or declare the detector uses `file_globs: ['**/*']` (no `languages:` key) so it passes the no-languages path (`return True, None`) — document which approach is chosen.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the detector is 'polyglot' but does not resolve a load-bearing routing constraint: `engine_b._is_applicable()` (lines 212–225) returns `(False, 'unsupported_lang')` for any language not in `_LANG_EXTENSIONS`. A `languages: [generic]` opengrep rule (the standard polyglot form) has no entry in that table, so `wanted` is empty and the detector is skipped as `unsupported_lang` — it never runs. The plan does not specify how to make the detector actually reach all file types.
findings.location: ## What — 'Detector is polyglot'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies the granularity post-filter behavior: a conflict marker present ONLY in unchanged lines of a diff does NOT produce a finding (or is explicitly accepted as a known false-positive if whole-file scope is chosen), and a marker in a changed line DOES produce a finding.
findings.finding: The match-granularity decision (whole-file + location-filter vs added-line) introduces a behavioral edge — a conflict marker in an UNCHANGED section of a changed file — that no AC verifies is correctly suppressed.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria #2 / 'Granularity note' section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion covering NUL byte / encoding-corruption detection (e.g. 'NUL bytes in a changed file produce an advisory finding; files without NUL bytes do not'), or explicitly drop NUL byte detection from the What section if it is out of scope.
findings.finding: NUL byte detection is described as a deliverable in the What section ('obvious encoding corruption (NUL bytes)') but no acceptance criterion covers it — there is no AC requiring that NUL bytes produce a finding or that NUL-byte detection is tested.
findings.location: What section ('obvious encoding corruption (NUL bytes)') vs Acceptance Criteria (no matching AC)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one recall AC for NUL-byte detection (a file with an embedded NUL byte in a changed section is flagged) and one no-fire AC (a binary/image file or a file with no NUL bytes is not flagged).
findings.finding: NUL-byte detection (named in scope) has no recall or no-fire acceptance criterion — it is a new behavior with zero test coverage called out in the plan.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria / Scope ('obvious encoding corruption (NUL bytes)')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ACs: '- [ ] A changed file containing a NUL byte produces an advisory finding; a changed binary file (e.g. PNG header) does NOT produce a spurious NUL finding (or document that binary files are explicitly excluded).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the matching strategy for '=======' (e.g., 'fire only when all three marker lines are present in the same file/hunk', or 'match the full git-conflict pattern ^<<<<<<< .*\n...^=======\n...^>>>>>>> .*', or 'accept ======= as a standalone pattern and document it as a known FP') so the executor does not have to choose the algorithm independently.
findings.finding: AC4 defers a real design decision to the executor: whether the detector must avoid firing on legitimate '=======' occurrences (e.g. inside heredocs or markdown tables) is stated as a requirement ('no-fire eval passes') but HOW this is achieved — context-aware parsing, anchored pattern requiring all three marker types, or accepted FP — is left completely unspecified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the 'What' section, e.g.: 'Match the full three-line conflict block pattern (<<<<<<< … ======= … >>>>>>>) co-present in a hunk to avoid FPs on standalone ======= in markdown/YAML; NUL bytes are matched independently.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the disambiguation rule for standalone '=======' (e.g., require all three markers to appear in the same file/hunk, or require the conflict-marker triplet pattern) so the plan body faithfully supports the no-false-positive claim in AC #4.
findings.finding: AC #4 promises a no-fire eval on a legitimate '=======' inside a string/heredoc, but the plan body describes no disambiguation mechanism — pure substring matching on '=======' will false-positive on Markdown horizontal rules, YAML block scalars, ASCII art separators, and many other legitimate uses.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC line 4: 'Recall+no-fire eval passes (marker present vs a legitimate '=======' inside a string/heredoc).'
findings.suggested_fix: Require the detector to match the *triplet pattern* (all three of <<<<<<< … ======= … >>>>>>> present in the same file or hunk) rather than any individual marker in isolation; document this in the plan body and add a multi-language fixture (Markdown, YAML, shell) to the eval suite.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the disambiguation rule for standalone `=======` so the mechanism supports the 'zero-FP' claim: either (a) require the conflict-marker *triplet* (`<<<<<<<` … `=======` … `>>>>>>>`) to co-occur in the same file/hunk, making `=======` alone insufficient to fire, or (b) match only `<<<<<<< ` and `>>>>>>> ` (with a trailing space — the canonical VCS format) and drop `=======` as a standalone signal.
findings.finding: The plan's core 'zero-FP' claim and AC #4 ('a legitimate `=======` inside a string/heredoc does not fire') are not mechanistically grounded. A pure substring or regex match on `=======` is high-FP: Markdown heading underlines (`=====` or `=======`), YAML block scalar separators, shell heredoc delimiters, ASCII art dividers, and RST section headers all produce standalone `=======` lines in legitimate committed code. The plan body describes no disambiguation mechanism — it only says 'drops the fuzzy dso parts' without explaining how `=======` is distinguished from non-conflict-marker uses. The existing no-FP rationale ('unambiguous, polyglot, zero-FP') applies only to `<<<<<<<` and `>>>>>>>` (which are nearly always conflict markers), not to standalone `=======`.
findings.location: What section — 'flags VCS conflict markers (<<<<<<<, =======, >>>>>>>)'; AC line 4 — 'Recall+no-fire eval passes (marker present vs a legitimate `=======` inside a string/heredoc)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For each AC, add a concrete proving command or test reference (e.g., `pytest tests/test_det_conflict_markers.py`, a specific fixture file path, or a named eval script) that produces pass/fail evidence in-session.
findings.finding: No proving command or test artifact is specified for any acceptance criterion. The plan names ACs but provides no concrete command (e.g. a pytest invocation, a CI job name, an eval script) that would produce evidence of success for any of the four criteria.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all four bullets)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Verification' section listing: (1) a unit-test file path with fixture diffs (one with markers, one clean, one with ======= in a heredoc), (2) the pytest/CI command to run them, and (3) a grep/config command confirming blocking_enabled=false in the routing entry.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite AC4 as a specific observable outcome, e.g. 'A unit test with at least two fixtures (one real conflict diff, one diff containing only ======= in a heredoc) passes in CI' so it is measurable and repeatable.
findings.finding: AC4's success condition 'Recall+no-fire eval passes' is not stated as a specific observable outcome — it does not specify what constitutes the eval (number of fixtures, a named test, pass/fail threshold), making it effort-framed rather than outcome-framed.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Recall+no-fire eval passes (marker present vs a legitimate ======= inside a string/heredoc)' with: 'A pytest test (tests/test_det_conflict_markers.py) with ≥2 fixture diffs — one triggering a finding, one clean — passes in CI with no false positives on the heredoc fixture.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a concrete documentation artifact (e.g., an ADR entry, a CLAUDE.md section, or an inline docstring in detectors.py) where the match-granularity decision (whole-file+location-filter vs added-line) and its rationale will be recorded.
findings.finding: The plan says to 'decide and document' the match-granularity architectural decision, but specifies no location where that decision will be recorded (no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc target).
findings.location: Scope section / AC line 2: 'The match-granularity decision (whole-file+location-filter vs added-line) is implemented + documented.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an ADR entry or a `## Granularity Decision` section in CLAUDE.md capturing: the two options considered, the chosen approach, and the false-positive implication (pre-existing markers in unchanged files will be filtered by location post-filter, not by added-line matching).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add tests/unit/test_code_review_ws2.py (or a dedicated test file) to the edit list to pin the new DET criterion's routing contract (blocking_enabled=false, fail_mode=open, detector id selector) analogous to how secret-detection and high-critical-security are pinned.
findings.finding: The plan does not enumerate tests/unit/test_code_review_ws2.py as a consumer that validates the criteria_routing.json DET structure. While the existing assertion (`assert 'secret-detection' in idx and 'high-critical-security' in idx`) is additive and won't break, this test file's preamble says it 'Pins the cross-story contracts (the WS5 blocking_enabled handoff)' — a new DET criterion may need a corresponding assertion added here to pin the new contract.
findings.location: ## Scope — 'criteria_routing.json entry (SHARED — coordinate with C, E)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document why the DET criterion is additive vs the existing `check-merge-conflict` pre-commit hook (`.pre-commit-config.yaml` line 19) — e.g. covers PR contributors who skip pre-commit, or CI diff review not covered by local hooks.
findings.finding: The plan motivates the DET criterion as a 'deterministic check' for conflict markers without acknowledging that the repo already has a `check-merge-conflict` pre-commit hook (`.pre-commit-config.yaml` line 19, `pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks`) that does exactly this. The plan must either explain why a code-review-gate DET criterion is additive/complementary to the pre-commit hook (e.g. it catches conflicts that slipped past pre-commit in CI, or covers PR-review of code from contributors without the hook installed) or acknowledge a duplication risk.
findings.location: ## Why / ## What
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the ## Why section: 'The existing `check-merge-conflict` pre-commit hook guards local commits; this DET criterion catches markers that slipped past the hook (e.g. from contributors without pre-commit installed, or merge-tool mishaps post-hook-run) at the code-review gate — a defense-in-depth layer, not a replacement.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-line note to the plan (or a comment in criteria_routing.json) identifying the owner of the DET routing entry and the expected cleanup action if the criterion is retired or superseded.
findings.finding: The retirement/cleanup path for the new criteria_routing.json entry and the builtin detector rule is unspecified; if the DET criterion is later deprecated or merged, the shared routing config will silently accumulate a stale entry.
findings.location: Scope section: 'A builtin detector rule + criteria_routing.json entry (SHARED — coordinate with C, E)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the reference to `apply_failclosed` with the fail-open requirement: either clarify that only the *scan* half of the Engine B pattern is reused (with blocking_enabled explicitly set to false), or rename the referenced pattern step to avoid implying fail-closed wiring.
findings.finding: The What section references the 'engine_b.scan -> apply_failclosed' consumer pattern by name, but the same section and AC3 declare the detector must be fail-open (blocking_enabled false). 'apply_failclosed' and 'fail-open' are contradictory: an implementer following the named pattern may wire blocking_enabled:true, violating the fail-open constraint.
findings.location: What section ('Reuses the Engine B detector consumer pattern (engine_b.scan -> apply_failclosed)') vs AC3 ('fail-open; no new BLOCK source')
findings.suggested_fix: Change the What section to: 'Reuses engine_b.scan for detection but wires the result with blocking_enabled:false (advisory only), not via apply_failclosed which implies fail-closed behavior.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether detectors.py requires any code change: if the new DET criterion follows the public-exposure-without-auth precedent (criteria_routing.json entry + detector YAML only), remove detectors.py from the edit list; if the added-line-scoping decision requires a code change there, name the specific function/block that changes.
findings.finding: The plan names 'detectors.py wiring' as an edit target but the data-driven routing seam (det_criteria() reads exec:DET entries from criteria_routing.json; criterion_for_detector() routes by selector) means code_review/detectors.py likely needs NO code change — the new DET entry in criteria_routing.json + new detector YAML is sufficient, exactly as the public-exposure-without-auth precedent demonstrates. The plan should clarify whether detectors.py actually changes or is just the run-time consumer.
findings.location: ## Scope — 'detectors.py wiring (and the added-line-scoping decision)'
findings.suggested_fix: Inspect src/rebar/llm/code_review/criteria_routing.json's public-exposure-without-auth entry (lines 98-107) — it is a full DET advisory criterion added with zero detectors.py changes. If conflict-marker detection follows the same pattern, remove detectors.py from scope and document that the data-driven seam handles routing automatically.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
